summary: Evidence anchoring for the dynamic coach's SENSOR-side interpretive thresholds (core/coach_state.py, with two controller constants in core/coach_plan.py). The load/recovery formulas are settled; this pack cites the numbers behind the bands and records the honest caveats. The research confirmed the provisional values rather than overturning them, so no engine value changed — what changed is provenance plus a drift guard (tests/test_coach_evidence.py asserts every 'constants' value here still equals the code). Grades: E1 strong (meta/RCT), E2 moderate, E3 practice-based, E4 contested. ACWR is E4-adjacent: surfaced as one signal with its critiques attached, never a verdict.

acwr.caveat: The 0.8–1.3 'sweet spot' / >1.5 'danger zone' is Gabbett's popularised model. It is genuinely contested: Lolli et al. show the conventional coupled ratio is mathematically coupled (acute is part of chronic), producing spurious correlation; Impellizzeri et al. argue there is no established causal basis for manipulating ACWR to reduce injury. We report it as one contextual signal with this caveat, never as a gate or a guarantee.

recommended.note: 3–5/week is the more conservative band for running (higher impact-injury risk); 8+ is where most athletes start breaking down.

ctl_ramp_rate.caveat: Practice-based (TrainingPeaks / practitioner consensus), not an RCT-backed constant. Individual tolerance varies with training history and injury proclivity; treated as a ceiling on build-goal targets, not a target.

tsb_bands.caveat: Form (TSB) interpretation bands are practitioner convention from the Performance Management Chart, not validated cut-offs. The coach uses the deep-negative floor to force recovery today; the other bands are advisory context.

readiness_rhr.caveat: Resting/submaximal heart rate is a validated fatigue-monitoring signal (Buchheit); the specific +5 bpm-over-baseline cut-off is a practice default. Ideally the threshold is the athlete's own smallest-worthwhile-change from a rolling baseline, not a fixed number.

recommended.monotony: mean(7d daily load) / SD(7d daily load), rest days included

monotony_strain.caveat: Foster's monotony/strain are reported by the sensor, not yet gated on. High monotony (commonly flagged around >2) combined with high load is the classic overtraining/illness pattern; the flag value is practitioner convention, not a hard threshold in Foster's original.

hard_day.caveat: A pragmatic definition with no direct citation: a day whose load is clearly above habitual fitness (≥ 1.5× CTL), floored so early-history days (tiny CTL) don't all read as hard. Used only to protect hard/easy spacing.

maintenance_dose.caveat: Informational — not yet a code constant. Fitness (VO2max) is largely held when intensity is maintained and frequency stays above ~80% of habitual, even as volume drops; measurable VO2max decline appears from ~2 weeks of training cessation (blood/plasma volume first). Intended to inform a future 'maintain' goal and detraining/return logic.

gabbett2016.title: The training—injury prevention paradox: should athletes be training smarter and harder?

lolli2019.authors: Lolli L, Batterham AM, Hawkins R, et al.
lolli2019.title: Mathematical coupling causes spurious correlation within the conventional acute-to-chronic workload ratio calculations

impellizzeri2020.authors: Impellizzeri FM, Tenan MS, Kempton T, Novak A, Coutts AJ
impellizzeri2020.title: Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio: Conceptual Issues and Fundamental Pitfalls
impellizzeri2020.venue: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance 15(6):907–913

foster1998.title: Monitoring training in athletes with reference to overtraining syndrome

buchheit2014.title: Monitoring training status with HR measures: do all roads lead to Rome?

mujika2000a.title: Detraining: loss of training-induced physiological and performance adaptations. Part I: short term insufficient training stimulus

mujika2000b.title: Detraining: loss of training-induced physiological and performance adaptations. Part II: long term insufficient training stimulus

couzens_ramp.title: CTL Ramp Rates, TSB floors & Loading Patterns

trainingpeaks_pmc.title: A Coach's Guide to ATL, CTL & TSB
